response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend a lot of time on social media every day, and honestly it stresses me out how much fake garbage is floating around out there. As someone raising two kids on my own after losing my wife, I worry constantly about what they might stumble across online. You cannot trust half of what you see anymore, and that is a real problem for families like mine. I work hard as a plumber, I do my job properly and with precision, and I expect the same standards from the information I consume. When I see people sharing nonsense without checking if it is even true, it frustrates me to no end. My approach is straightforward and maybe not popular with everyone, but I do not care much about being liked. First, I stick to sources I know and trust. I am not interested in exploring every new platform or trendy account. I follow local Bologna news, my church community page, and a few cycling groups. That is it. I do not need more chaos in my feed. When something seems off or too dramatic, I simply do not share it. Period. I have seen too many people, even in my own family, spread fake health advice or political rumors without thinking about the consequences. My parents were educated people, they taught me to be careful even if I did not go far in school myself. Second, I report fake content when I see it, especially anything that could harm kids. The platforms need to do better, but until they do, I take it upon myself. I also limit my children's access strictly because I cannot monitor everything, and that worry keeps me up at night sometimes. The anxiety of raising them right in this digital mess is real. I wish people would just think before they post and stop treating social media like it does not matter. It does matter. Every lie shared makes it harder for honest people to know what is real, and in my line of work and life, I have learned that precision and truth matter more than being the first to share something sensational.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 29-year-old widowed father of two with high conscientiousness, high neuroticism, low agreeableness, and low openness. He spends several hours daily on social media but lacks formal education. His high conscientiousness suggests he values order, precision, and responsibility. His high neuroticism indicates anxiety and worry, especially regarding his children. Low agreeableness means he is direct, possibly blunt, and less concerned with social harmony. Low openness suggests preference for familiar routines and skepticism toward new ideas. As a plumber in Bologna, he values practical, hands-on competence. His Christian beliefs and family responsibilities shape his moral framework. The tone should be direct, somewhat anxious, practical, and skeptical. He would approach fake content with worry about his children, frustration at carelessness, preference for trusted familiar sources, and a no-nonsense attitude. Examples should reference his work ethic, parenting concerns, Bologna community, and his hobbies as stress relief.
response_parsed.tone: direct, anxious, practical, skeptical, blunt, worried, conscientious
